data.title: SLB sees Q4 revenue surpassing $10B while targeting Data Center Solutions run rate exceeding $2B by end-2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SLB (SLB) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Olivier Le Peuch said Q2 was “a solid quarter for SLB, marked by broad-based international growth and rebound in North America,” while noting the Middle East remained disrupted: “operations in Iraq remained constrained by security challenge.”
  • Le Peuch highlighted momentum in Production Systems and Digital, tying demand to customers’ focus on “improving production, enhancing recovery and extending the life of existing assets.” He added, “Production Systems adjusted EBITDA margins returned to above 20%,” and said ChampionX “delivered sequential margin expansion for the third consecutive quarter” despite chemical cost inflation.
  • Le Peuch said Digital had a strong quarter with exploration data strength and ARR growth, adding, “Digital adjusted EBITDA margins to reach approximately 35% for the quarter. Additionally, annual recurring revenue increased by 15% year-over-year.”
  • On Data Center Solutions, Le Peuch said revenue “increasing 33% sequentially and 80% year-on-year,” and that backlog is “growing ahead of expectations.” He added: “we now foresee that Data Center Solutions will exit 2027 at an annualized revenue run rate exceeding $2 billion.”
  • CFO Stephane Biguet reported: “Second quarter earnings per share excluding charges and credits was $0.55,” and “our second quarter revenue of $9 billion increased 3% sequentially, despite severe disruptions in the Middle East.”

Outlook

  • Le Peuch guided Q3 on a base case of Middle East recovery: “we expect global sequential revenue growth between 3% and 4%, with adjusted EBITDA margin expansion of approximately 75 basis points,” and said core divisions should grow “in the low to mid-single digits,” while “Digital revenue is expected to grow in the low single digits.”
  • Le Peuch provided a downside scenario tied to renewed conflict: “we estimate third quarter revenue will be approximately $150 million lower than our base case assumption,” translating into “an adjusted EBITDA headwind of approximately $75 million,” concentrated in “Well Construction and Reservoir Performance.”
  • For Q4, Le Peuch said SLB’s preliminary view assumes Middle East activity “between $2.1 billion and $2.2 billion,” which he described as “approximately 95% of the revenue achieved in the fourth quarter of 2025,” and added: “we would expect fourth quarter revenue to surpass $10 billion” with “adjusted EBITDA margin to be approximately 24%.”
  • Compared with Q1’s more conditional framing (“it is challenging to provide precise guidance for this quarter”), Q2 included explicit Q3 growth and margin-expansion expectations plus a quantified downside scenario.

Financial Results

  • Biguet said Middle East disruption weighed on results but was offset by other geographies: “Strong performance in Latin America, Europe and Africa, U.S. land and Asia more than offset the decline in the Middle East where revenue fell 13% sequentially to $1.66 billion.”
  • Biguet said profitability improved sequentially: “our pretax segment operating margin increased 49 basis points sequentially and our adjusted EBITDA margin increased 83 basis points sequentially,” adding the company used “temporary cost actions” in the Middle East.
  • By division, Biguet reported Digital revenue of “$697 million,” Reservoir Performance revenue of “$1.6 billion,” Well Construction revenue of “$2.7 billion,” and Production Systems revenue of “$3.8 billion.”
  • Biguet described cash flow and capital return: “We generated $1.4 billion of cash flow from operations and free cash flow of $716 million,” ended with “net debt of $8.7 billion,” and said SLB repurchased “$648 million” of stock while still targeting “to return more than $4 billion to our shareholders in 2026.”

Q&A

  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup: Asked about the bridge to Q4 revenue surpassing $10 billion and whether Q4 is a reasonable 2027 run-rate; CEO Le Peuch responded, “I will not comment at this point on ’27,” and described Q4 drivers as “the further Middle East recovery,” “the usual year-end sales into Digital and Production Systems,” and “Data Center,” while CFO Biguet clarified Q2 Middle East revenue was “$1.66 billion” and reiterated the Q3 downside case could bring Middle East revenue “more or less the level of Q2.”
  • Scott Gruber, Citigroup: Asked about exploration durability; CEO Le Peuch said exploration/appraisal is “not being a trend of 1 quarter, but a long-term trend,” citing reserve replacement and energy security, and referenced offerings including “Reservoir Performance wireline,” Digital exploration data, and “AlphaSight.”
  • James West, Melius Research: Pressed on urgency and sequencing of Middle East recovery work; CEO Le Peuch said customer engagement is increasing to “secure mobilization resource,” with early vectors including “production recovery… well intervention… ChampionX capability,” plus Digital as “a new catalyst.”
  • John Anderson, Barclays: Asked about offshore acceleration into 2027 and timing of Middle East restoration; CEO Le Peuch said deepwater activity should “grow directionally well into next year,” reiterated an ambition that “our subsea booking will reach $9 billion over 2 years,” and cautioned “it will not be prudent to assume that the things will restore in weeks.”
  • Neil Mehta, Goldman Sachs: Asked what SLB provides in Data Center Solutions and economics; CEO Le Peuch described off-site modular delivery for “server hall” and “cooling equipment,” and CFO Biguet said the business is “not accretive to SLB’s overall margins” but “very accretive to top line growth… earnings growth” with “very strong free cash flow generation.”
  • Keith MacKey, RBC: Asked about Venezuela and revenue potential; CEO Le Peuch referenced scaling under an “OFAC license,” and said SLB expects “significant scale-up… during the next few months” to shape “2027 in a significant growth curve,” while noting prior peak staffing and revenue: “more than 3,000 people” and “more than $1 billion.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical on geopolitical and timing assumptions, including Barclays calling SLB’s view “a little bit controversial,” and repeated probing on recovery speed, offshore growth, and scaling constraints in data centers.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive with conditional caution on the Middle East, using phrases such as “base case assumes a gradual recovery,” while also expressing confidence in strategy execution: CEO Le Peuch said the market is “beginning to exhibit the characteristics of an upcycle” and “we are confident” in Digital growth drivers.
  • Versus Q1, management maintained a conflict-focused posture but shifted toward more quantified outlook framing (base case, downside scenario, and preliminary Q4 ranges) and emphasized improved sequential financial delivery.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q2 highlighted “rebound in U.S. land” and “broad-based international growth,” while Q1 described “a challenging start of the year” with “severe disruption in the Middle East that impacted our first quarter revenue and earnings.”
  • Data Center Solutions expectations stepped up from Q1’s “on track to exit the year at a $1 billion run rate” to Q2’s “exit 2027… exceeding $2 billion,” alongside new partnership references including “the recent announcement with Meta.”
  • Q2 guidance language became more specific, with explicit Q3 revenue growth and margin expansion ranges plus a quantified downside case; Q1 centered on a scenario framework for Q2 without a global growth range.

Risks and Concerns

  • Le Peuch said the Middle East recovery path remains uncertain: “returning to full activity will take time and the pace of recovery will vary by country,” with Iraq “constrained by security challenge.”
  • Management cited logistics and cost pressures easing over time; Le Peuch said SLB is “learning how to readjust… the logistics and the supply” to “avoid some of the excessive costs.”
  • Pricing was described as a 2026 headwind: Le Peuch said pricing has been “generally speaking, globally a headwind in 2026,” especially in “large competitive tenders.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q2 as resilient performance despite Middle East disruption, with sequential improvements in revenue, margins, and free cash flow, and emphasized three forward drivers for investor focus: a quantified Q3 base case with a defined downside tied to geopolitical volatility, a preliminary Q4 view calling for revenue surpassing $10 billion with approximately 24% adjusted EBITDA margin, and an expanded “beyond the core” growth narrative led by Digital and Data Center Solutions, including an expectation to exit 2027 with a Data Center Solutions annualized revenue run rate exceeding $2 billion.

data.title: Flagstar forecasts $0.40-$0.50 EPS for 2026 while authorizing a $250M buyback amid elevated CRE payoffs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Flagstar Bank, National Association (FLG) Q2 2026

Management view

  • CEO & Executive Chairman Joseph Otting said, "We announced a $250 million share buyback," adding it was "a clear signal of progress" alongside "our third consecutive quarter of profitability" and an "inflection point" as "the balance sheet grew by almost $600 million."
  • Otting tied the quarter’s operating narrative to loan mix and credit trends, citing "a record level of C&I loan production," "solid deposit growth, all while reducing our deposit costs," and "the continued strategic reduction in the commercial real estate loan portfolio."
  • Chief Banking Officer, Co-President & Co-COO Richard Raffetto said, "we generated $4.2 billion in new and increased credit commitments," producing "$2.8 billion in new C&I closed loan originations," with a "pipeline going into the third quarter" of "over $2 billion in C&I commitments" and "75 new to bank C&I relationships."
  • CFO, Co-President & Co-COO Lee Smith said, "We reported net income attributable to common stockholders of $0.06 per diluted share on a GAAP basis and $0.05 per diluted share on an adjusted basis," and highlighted "operating expenses" down "to $427 million" and a "13.16% CET1 capital ratio."

Outlook

  • Smith said, "We have adjusted our interest income guidance downward for both years as a result of increased multi-family and CRE payoffs, paydowns and amortization," and added that deposit mix shifted because "the timing has been pushed out" for noninterest-bearing DDAs.
  • Smith guided, "EPS for '26 is now forecast to be in the $0.40 to $0.50 range and EPS for '27 is forecast to be in the $1.60 to $1.70 range," and noted "non-accrual loan balances at the end of the year are expected to be slightly higher than previously forecasted."
  • Versus the prior quarter’s guidance, Q2 lowered the EPS ranges (Q1: "$0.60 to $0.65" for 2026 and "$1.80 to $1.90" for 2027) and changed the balance sheet outlook (Q1: "approximately $94 billion" at end of 2026 and "$102 billion" at end of 2027; Q2: "about $91.5 billion to $92 billion" at end of 2026 and "$100 billion" by end of 2027).

Financial results

  • Smith said, "pre-provision net revenue increased $34 million on an unadjusted basis" and expenses were "well within our previously provided guidance range," while Otting said "pre-provision net revenue increased 51% compared to last quarter."
  • Smith reported "The second quarter NIM of 2.13%" (and, "Excluding this, the net interest margin would have been 2.16%"), adding "June net interest margin was 2.19%" as the balance sheet expanded.
  • On portfolio repositioning, Smith said "multi-family and commercial real estate payoffs were again elevated during the quarter at $1.5 billion," and CRE concentration fell to "350%" (Otting: "350% from 367% last quarter").
  • On credit, Smith said "Non-accrual loans increased modestly to $2.8 billion," and net charge-offs were "$99 million" but "$47 million of that was already fully reserved for"; Otting said charge-offs increased to "$100 million," "but half of those were previously 100% reserved for."

Q&A

  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: asked how the bank balances growth vs. buybacks; CEO & Executive Chairman Otting said decisions hinge on "3 variables" including "growth in core earnings," "trends... in the credit quality," and "the amount of capital that we'll need to support the C&I growth."
  • Chiaverini, Jefferies: asked if C&I growth can continue; Chief Banking Officer Raffetto said, "we see consistent loan growth going forward, consistent with what we delivered in the second quarter."
  • David Rochester, Cantor: asked if buyback could expand; Otting reiterated the "3 variables" and said CET1 was "down this quarter because of the expansion of the balance sheet."
  • Rochester, Cantor: asked if "2.19%" June NIM is a floor; CFO Smith said, "I am looking at that as a floor."
  • Casey Haire: asked about NPL reduction and charge-offs; Smith said year-end non-accruals are expected at "about $2.3 billion" and referenced net charge-offs of "$99 million" with "$47 million" reserved.
  • Haire: asked reserve on new C&I; Smith said, "new C&I is coming on at about 1%."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: asked about loan yields; Smith said payoff yields were "just over 5%" and added, "this should sort of be a bottom, a floor what you saw in the second quarter."
  • Bernard Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank: asked rate-hike sensitivities; Smith said the forecast assumes "one rate hike" and borrowers may "wait to the last minute."
  • Von Gizycki, Deutsche Bank: asked balance sheet targets; Smith said end-2026 is "about $91.5 billion to $92 billion" and end-2027 "$100 billion."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked if expenses are flexible; Smith said, "we feel good about the guidance" while continuing to "invest in Rich's business."
  • Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: asked about deposits/fees from C&I; Raffetto cited "net loan growth of over $3 billion" and deposit growth of "$1.4 billion" year-to-date and said, "We expect our capital markets fees and treasury management fees... to show significant growth in 2026 and beyond."
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: asked if NII revisions are done; Otting said, "I think we feel pretty good," while Smith added, "Everything we can control, I think we're delivering on."
  • McGratty, KBW: asked if guidance includes buybacks; Smith said, "it's without it."
  • Benjamin Gerlinger, Citi: asked buyback price sensitivity; Otting said at $10, "that would be an incredibly attractive price."
  • David Smith, Truist: asked rent-freeze litigation impacts; Otting said there was "a slight uptick in the ACL" for rent-regulated multi-family and said the process supported that reserves are "satisfactory."
  • Timur Braziler, UBS: asked NIM path and retention; Smith said margin "continues to improve quarter-over-quarter" and retention is "about 35% to 40%."
  • Matthew Breese, Stephens: asked spot deposit cost; Smith said "it's about 2.49%" and said lowering costs "gets a little tougher" without rate cuts.
  • Breese, Stephens: pressed on multi-year rent freezes; Smith said prior analysis showed a "demarcation line was 70%" and for higher-regulated buildings NOI impact was "about 7% to 8%."
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: asked composition of NPL reduction and deposit-cost assumptions; Smith said NPL reductions are not "linear" and said deposit costs should be "relatively flat even with the rate hike."
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan: challenged buyback logic; Otting said, "I don't see that necessarily being a governor on the buybacks."
  • Elian, JPMorgan: asked fee drivers; Smith cited "capital markets and syndication fee income" and Rich Raffetto added "very good interest rate hedging opportunities" and "service charges on deposits."
  • Manuel Navas, Piper Sandler: asked talent competition and NII wildcards; Raffetto said the platform is "very attractive" and expects "another 20 to 30" hires; Smith cited payoffs, deposit mix, non-accrual resolution, and gain-on-sale sensitivity as key variables.
  • McGratty, KBW: asked tax rate; Smith said marginal is "26.5%" and back half "somewhere between the 26.5% and the 28.2."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to skeptical on the durability of NII and capital return, with questions like McGratty’s "Is this the last revision?" and Elian’s comment, "that doesn't sound like a recipe to deploy much of the buyback."
  • Management tone was slightly positive to confident in strategy execution, including Otting’s "we are confident that we are on the right path" and Smith’s "we feel good about the guidance that we've obviously put out this quarter."
  • Versus the prior quarter, management confidence remained similar but guidance language shifted more cautious, with Q2 explicitly saying guidance was "adjusted... downward" and noting DDAs and non-accrual expectations were "pushed out" or "slightly higher than previously forecasted."

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 introduced a new capital return action as Otting said, "we announced a $250 million share buyback," while Q1 said, "We expect the Board taking action on capital distributions in the second half of the year."
  • The growth narrative strengthened in C&I, with Raffetto reporting record production and Otting highlighting "$2 billion of net C&I loan growth," compared with Q1’s "$1.4 billion" net C&I growth and comments that Q2 fundings would be "similar to Q1."
  • Credit tone became more mixed in Q2, with Smith reporting non-accruals "increased modestly" to "$2.8 billion," compared with Q1’s "Nonaccrual loans totaled $2.7 billion, down $323 million or 11%."

Risks and concerns

  • Smith said elevated payoffs are "both good news and bad news" because they "reduce interest income and NIM in the short term," and he cited risks from "higher for longer" rates pressuring mortgage gain-on-sale.
  • Management highlighted rent-regulated New York City multi-family uncertainty, with Otting referencing the rent board decision that "ultimately voted not to increase rents for the 1- and 2-year leases," and Smith describing ongoing monitoring using annual financials, liens/violations, and an 18-month forward review.

Final takeaway

Management emphasized that Q2 combined a $250M buyback authorization with a quarter they described as an inflection in balance sheet growth, led by record C&I production and continued CRE de-risking, while also acknowledging that faster CRE and multi-family runoff and slower timing for noninterest-bearing DDA growth drove a downward reset to 2026–2027 interest income and EPS guidance.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Packaging Corp. surges to record high on report of containerboard price hike
data.description:
Packaging Corp. of America (PKG) up 8.1% to a record high in Friday's trading after a Truist Securities report said the company plans to raise containerboard prices by $140/ton, effective September 1.

"While this increase may be a stretch, our sources also suggested that another $50-$70/ton could likely be implemented," Truist analyst Michael Roxland said, believing the boost is being driven by higher costs, including freight, recycled fiber, chemicals, and purchased electricity, as well as "strong demand amid a tight supply situation."

The move also is positive for Buy-rated International Paper (IP) and Smurfit Westrock (SW), the analyst said; shares are up 10.1% and 10.8%, respectively.

data.title: First Hawaiian outlines 3%-4% loan growth and 3.24%-3.25% NIM outlook while advancing TriCo deal
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Hawaiian, Inc. (FHB) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “I would like to start with my excitement about our recently announced deal with TriCo Bancshares, and I'm looking forward to working with the TriCo team to build a leading Pacific banking franchise.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Robert Harrison)
  • “We had a strong start to the year. Loans grew, retail and commercial deposits were down slightly as expected. Credit quality remains solid, and we remain well capitalized.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • “Total loans grew $137 million in the quarter or about 3.6% on an annualized basis. Growth was led by C&I and CRE loans, partially offset by payoffs in the construction portfolio and lower residential loans as payoffs exceeded production.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • “Total deposits were down $623 million, with most of that decline due to outflows of public deposits.” (Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO James Moses)
  • “Net interest income was $171 million, $3.5 million more than the prior quarter. The NIM in the second quarter was 3.25%, up 6 basis points from the prior quarter.” (Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO Moses)
  • “The quarter included $4.2 million of expenses related to the TriCo transaction. Now we expect to incur more of those expenses in the back half of the year as we move to close on integration.” (Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO Moses)
  • “The bank continued to maintain its strong credit performance and healthy credit metrics in the second quarter.” (Executive VP & Chief Risk Officer Lea Nakamura)

Outlook

  • “We continue to expect full year loan growth to be in the 3% to 4% range.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • “With the markets now expecting one rate increase later this year, we have revised our full year NIM outlook to be in the 3.24% to 3.25% range. We also expect the third quarter NIM to be about 3.27%.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • “Our outlook for noninterest income remains unchanged at about $220 million for the year.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • “We expect reported expenses to be between $515 million and $520 million, excluding expenses related to the TriCo transaction.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)

Financial Results

  • “Our return on average tangible assets [was] 1.28% and a return on average tangible equity of 16.34% for the quarter.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • “Noninterest income was $60.3 million, primarily due to higher BOLI income, an excise tax refund and higher swap fees.” (Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO Moses)
  • “Noninterest expense in the second quarter was $130.4 million.” (Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO Moses)
  • “Our noninterest-bearing deposit ratio was 32%.” (Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO Moses)

Q&A

  • Kelly Motta, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: Can you discuss what drove the government deposit decline and core retail/commercial trends into the back half? Vice Chairman Moses: “This was not about loss of relationships or anything.”
  • Kelly Motta, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: How is pricing competition in loans and deposits with the Fed on hold/possible hike? Vice Chairman Moses: “We are seeing the same type of competition that we've always seen.”
  • Kelly Motta, Keefe, Bruyette, & Woods, Inc., Research Division: What drives loan growth in the back half? Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison: “We still see a very robust pipeline in both C&I and CRE.”
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: What’s behind the higher full-year NIM range? Vice Chairman Moses: “I think it really is just a change in outlook on the macro side of things that's driving an update to our NIM.”
  • Anthony Elian, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division: How should investors think about buybacks with CET1 above 13% and the TriCo close? Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison: “We're probably not going to do buybacks throughout the rest of the year.”
  • Andrew Terrell, Stephens Inc., Research Division: Why could expenses step up in the back half? Vice Chairman Moses: “We're going to continue to hire people.”
  • Jared David Shaw, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: What drove higher BOLI income? Vice Chairman Moses: “That was a market impact on our BOLI this quarter.”
  • Timothy Mitchell, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: How has deal reception been, and how independent will TriCo be? Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison: “One of the reasons we like TriCo so much is they have a strong management team, and we're planning on keeping most of them there.”
  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler & Co., Research Division: What was the deposit cost spot rate at end of June? Vice Chairman Moses: “It was 1.21%.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, centered on deposit volatility, NIM assumptions under a possible hike, expense cadence, and TriCo integration specifics, including questions like “how should we think about buybacks as you work through the TriCo deal close?” (Anthony Elian, JPMorgan Chase & Co, Research Division)
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in deal-related Q&A, with tighter language on capital deployment such as “We're probably not going to do buybacks throughout the rest of the year.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • Versus last quarter, management’s prepared remarks shifted from macro uncertainty language (“it's too soon to know how tourism and the local economy might be impacted by the recent global events.”) (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison, Q1) to transaction-focused emphasis (“recently announced deal with TriCo Bancshares”). (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison, Q2)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language moved with rate expectations: in Q1, “markets now expecting no rate cuts this year” with full-year NIM “3.22% to 3.23%”; in Q2, “markets now expecting one rate increase later this year” with full-year NIM “3.24% to 3.25%.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • Capital actions shifted alongside the pending deal: Q1 included “repurchased about 1.3 million shares at a cost of $32 million,” while Q2 noted “we did not purchase any shares” and “it's expected to close near the end of the year.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • Deposit discussion flipped from Q1 strength (“total deposits increasing by $262 million”) to Q2 normalization (“Total deposits were down $623 million... due to outflows of public deposits”), with management framing the Q2 move as expected seasonality and balance volatility. (Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO Moses)

Risks and Concerns

  • “Cash balances were lower in Q2, primarily due to the decline in public deposit balances.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)
  • “We expect to incur more of those expenses in the back half of the year as we move to close on integration.” (Vice Chairman of Finance Group & CFO Moses)
  • “The residential side continues to slow given the rate environment.” (Chairman of the Board, President & CEO Harrison)

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a Q2 profitability lift alongside updated full-year targets of 3%-4% loan growth and a 3.24%-3.25% NIM outlook that assumes one rate increase later in the year, while positioning the pending TriCo Bancshares transaction as the central strategic development. Management also signaled a pause in buybacks into year-end and highlighted that integration-related expenses are expected to rise in the back half of the year as closing work ramps.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SpaceX in focus as Morgan Stanley ponders 'zero' AI value; Musk previews new models
data.description:
SpaceX (SPCX) was in the spotlight on Friday as Morgan Stanley said that a share price of $100 would mean the tech giant has “zero” value related to artificial intelligence.

Separately, CEO Elon Musk said new Grok models were coming shortly. 

Reiterating his Overweight rating and $300 price target on SpaceX shares, Morgan Stanley analyst Adam Jonas said the “current disconnect” between bearish sentiment and largely unchanged fundamentals provides an attractive entry point for investors.

“Despite no major fundamental changes to the story over the past few weeks, bears appear to have become gradually more convicted, while bulls appear more resigned to wait for more obvious positive catalysts,” Jonas wrote in a note to clients. “Most investors we speak with significantly discount Grok & Cursor and view the bulk of the AI monetization opportunity as neocloud. Many ascribe zero or even negative value for AI given the high capex requirements relative to Space & Connectivity, largely uncertain economics, and the high degree of management time devoted to the business.”

Jonas added that shares could drop further in the near term, citing the impending lockup  expiration from insiders, as well as its upcoming earnings report, set for Aug. 4, which he believes could “leave many key investor questions unanswered.”

“At less than $120/share today, we believe investors are paying a reasonable multiple for the core Space + Connectivity business while receiving limited-to-no value for the company’s AI opportunity, despite multiple potential monetization paths to our estimated ~$100bn in 2028 AI revenues on 6 GW avg compute,” Jonas added. “We see the current valuation as an attractive entry point, with our $300 price target implying substantial upside and supported by a 15-year DCF that values Space + Connectivity alone at approximately $136/share.”

New Grok models coming, potential catalysts

Despite the weak investor sentiment, Jonas said SpaceX has several positive catalysts coming, including the launch of new Grok models.

On Friday, Musk posted to X that Grok 4.6 would be released in two weeks, while Grok 4.7 would be coming in four weeks.

Other possible catalysts for the stock include the 14th and 15th test flights of its Starship rocket (which could also feature the first catch of the V3 Starship Booster and an orbital Starship flight); updates on Cursor's annual recurring revenue and Grok adoption; new neocloud deals; and the possible release of Grok 5.0 before the end of 2026.

data.title: Outlook gains approval for ophthalmic bevacizumab for wet AMD
data.description:
• The US FDA has approved Outlook Therapeutics' (OTLK) Lytenava (bevacizumab-vikg) for wet AMD.
  • Lytevana is the first ophthalmic bevacizumab approved for the condition.
  • The standard formulation of bevacizumab is marketed by Roche (RHHBY) as Avastin.
  • The drug is administered once a month.

data.title: HCA projects $77B-$79.5B 2026 revenue amid (-$1B)-(-$1.2B) exchange headwind
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: HCA Healthcare (HCA) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “The enhanced premium tax credits expired at the end of the year and the effects, as expected, were that many people became uninsured and still needed emergency care from hospitals,” said (CEO & Director Samuel Hazen), adding, “the impact was greater than our estimates.”
  • “Overall, adjusted admissions for patients who were formerly covered by the health insurance exchanges declined by 15%,” said (CEO Hazen), and “these patients migrated almost one-for-one to uninsured.”
  • “Despite the payer mix shift, we were pleased with our volume growth,” said (CEO Hazen), citing that “Emergency room visits, cardiac procedures and rehab volumes helped drive these improvements,” while “the primary explanation for the decline was from reduced demand in elective surgeries.”
  • “We have approved more than $7 billion in capital expenditures that should come online in the next 3 years,” said (CEO Hazen), and “we expect to use our cash flow and balance sheet strength to invest further in our business while also returning capital to our shareholders.”
  • “The significant payer mix shift related to the exchanges has had an unfavorable impact on adjusted EBITDA of approximately $400 million,” said (CFO & Executive VP Mike Marks).
  • “During the second quarter, the company recognized approximately $400 million of incremental net benefit from Medicaid Supplemental Payment Programs,” said (CFO Marks), including “a $540 million incremental net benefit related to the recently approved Florida program from October 1, 2024, to June 30, 2026.”

Outlook

  • “Our revised 2026 guidance ranges” are “revenue between $77 billion and $79.5 billion” and “diluted earnings per share between $28.70 and $30.50,” said (CFO Marks).
  • “We also included revised key assumptions” of “health insurance exchanges between a negative $1 billion and $1.2 billion” and “Medicaid Supplemental Payment Program net benefit between $300 million and $500 million,” said (CFO Marks).
  • “The key change in our updated estimate is driven by our evaluation that almost all of the individuals losing coverage on the exchanges are becoming uninsured versus our original assumption of 80% to 85%,” said (CFO Marks), adding, “our original assumption around declining utilization for patients that become uninsured... did not materialize.”
  • “We believe the fourth quarter adjusted EBITDA growth rate compared to the prior year may be higher than for the third quarter,” said (CFO Marks), “based on our assumptions around the timing effects of exchanges, Medicaid Supplemental Payment Programs and our resiliency program.”

Financial results

  • “Admissions increased 2.5% and equivalent admissions increased 2.7%,” said (CFO Marks), and “ER visits increased 3.6%.”
  • “Inpatient surgeries were down 2.3%, outpatient surgeries were down 3.4%,” said (CFO Marks).
  • “Capital expenditures totaled $1.2 billion in the quarter,” said (CFO Marks), and “we purchased... $2.064 billion of our outstanding shares, and we paid $171 million in dividends.”
  • “Cash flow from operations was $2.3 billion in the quarter, which is a 45% decline from prior year quarter,” said (CFO Marks), attributing it “primarily due to timing differences... related to Florida's Medicaid Supplemental Payment Program as well as the prior year deferral of federal income tax payments.”

Q&A

  • Benjamin Hendrix, RBC Capital Markets: “more color on your increased estimate for exchange headwinds”; (CFO Marks): “our data is telling us now that it's closer to one-for-one.”
  • Albert Rice, UBS: “drill down a little bit on surgeries”; (CEO Hazen): “this year, we're down on elective 6%... we do believe that HICS demand... is a part of it.”
  • Ann Hynes, Mizuho: “talk about the competitive environment... gaining market share”; (CEO Hazen): “our competitive positioning has -- is stable to growing net-net.”
  • Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies: “OpEx -- other OpEx line was up”; (CFO Marks): “other operating expenses are being inflated because of the provider tax.”
  • Pito Chickering, Deutsche Bank: “help bridge us how you get to your guidance”; (CFO Marks): “The first one is really volume... The second is our cost.”
  • Matthew Gillmor, KeyBanc: “3 divisions represented 50% of the impact”; (CEO Hazen): “our Gulf Coast division, North Florida and South Atlantic division.”
  • Benjamin Mayo, Leerink: “work requirements for 2027”; (CFO Marks): “we beefed up those teams... and we are preparing the best we can.”
  • Justin Lake, Wolfe: “first quarter versus second quarter” and “volume growth by payer”; (CFO Marks): “Commercial, excluding the exchanges are up 2.4%... the total uninsured is up 15%.”
  • Stephen Baxter, Wells Fargo: “attrition rates for the exchanges in 2027”; (CEO Hazen): “the loss of coverage will be less than 2026.”
  • Andrew Mok, Barclays: “how many quarters worth of Florida DPP were recognized”; (CFO Marks): “that's 21 months' worth of benefit booked into the second quarter.”
  • Ryan Langston, TD Cowen: “monthly cadence... and... proposed OPPS rule for '27”; (CFO Marks): “We don't comment about mid-quarter progression... we are generally pleased.”
  • Scott Fidel, Goldman Sachs: “IDR claims from the No Surprises Act”; (CEO Hazen): “we have very few of our accounts go through the IDR process.”
  • Kevin Fischbeck, BofA Securities: “other $450 million” in the guidance reduction; (CFO Marks): “that really reflects a bit of the moderation in our growth rates.”

Sentiment analysis

  • “What’s giving you confidence” and “still not clear to me” (Hendrix; Fischbeck) reflected slightly negative analyst sentiment centered on exchange headwinds and the EBITDA bridge.
  • “we are pleased,” “we believe,” and “we were pleased with our cost results” (CEO Hazen; CFO Marks) reflected slightly positive management sentiment, with repeated caveats that “The variables on the exchanges are difficult to predict.”
  • Versus Q1’s tone that exchange shifts were “generally in line with our expectations” (CEO Hazen, Q1), Q2 language shifted to “the impact was greater than our estimates” (CEO Hazen, Q2).

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • In Q1, management said, “the underlying shifts resulting from the changes in the health insurance exchanges were generally in line with our expectations,” while in Q2 management said, “these patients migrated almost one-for-one to uninsured,” and updated exchange impact assumptions to “a negative $1 billion and $1.2 billion.”
  • Q1 highlighted weather/seasonal disruption (“respiratory-related admissions were down 42%”) while Q2 centered on coverage loss and mix (“enhanced premium tax credits expired”).
  • Q1 guidance was “reaffirming our estimated guidance ranges for 2026,” while Q2 introduced “our revised 2026 guidance ranges” and framed them as “more in line with our long-term adjusted EBITDA growth rate target of 4% to 6%.”

Risks and concerns

  • “We expected some of these patients to shift to other forms of coverage, but this did not happen,” said (CEO Hazen), and “it does put pressure on the P&L.”
  • “This one-for-one migration makes up approximately 80% of our uninsured volume growth,” said (CFO Marks), and the remainder was tied to “a decrease in Medicaid conversions, mostly in Texas.”
  • “We believe work requirements will have an impact,” said (CFO Marks), and mitigation included “coverage benefit support teams... embedded in all of our facilities.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q2 around stronger-than-expected exchange-to-uninsured migration following the expiration of enhanced premium tax credits, partially offset by Medicaid supplemental payments (including Florida) and ongoing cost initiatives under the financial resiliency program. Updated full-year guidance incorporated a larger exchange-related adjusted EBITDA headwind, while management emphasized continued demand in several service lines, ongoing network capacity expansion, and a capital allocation posture that maintained sizable CapEx and share repurchases alongside dividends.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Roku lifts prices for streaming devices, citing memory chip shortage
data.description:
Roku (ROKU) has increased prices across its streaming hardware lineup, with some devices seeing price hikes of as much as $50 as the company grapples with the ongoing global memory shortage.

The steepest price increases are for the Roku Ultra and Roku Streambar SE, both of which now retail for $149.99, up from $99.99. The Streaming Stick 4K now costs $79.99, compared with $49.99 previously, while the Streaming Stick Plus has risen to $59.99 from $39.99. Roku's entry-level Streaming Stick now sells for $39.99, up from $29.99.

The company is currently offering temporary discounts on some models, bringing their prices back to previous levels for a limited period.

A Roku (ROKU) executive told industry publication The Desk that the higher prices were driven by the continuing shortage of memory chips, which is expected to persist through 2027 and beyond. The supply crunch has been exacerbated by chipmakers prioritizing production for artificial intelligence applications, tightening availability for consumer electronics manufacturers.

The move reflects broader cost pressures across the technology industry, where companies have been grappling with rising memory prices. Apple (AAPL) also increased prices for several products, including its Apple TV streaming device, earlier this year, citing similar market conditions.

The price revisions come weeks after Fox Corporation (FOX) announced plans to acquire Roku (ROKU), as the streaming platform company navigates higher hardware costs alongside a major ownership change.

data.title: Catalyst Watch: Tech majors report, FOMC, Jersey Mike's IPO and more oil anxiety
data.description:
Welcome to Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch - a breakdown of some of next week's actionable events that stand out. Read more about the events that may impact stock prices next week.

 

Monday - July 27

  • Volatility watch - Netflix (NFLX) and Carvana (CVNA)  have seen options volatility increase over the last week. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Inno Holdings (INHD), Forte Biosciences Inc (FBRX), and Utz Brands (UTZ). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Spero Therapeutics (SPRO), Shutterstock (SSTK), and FlyExclusive (FLYX). Short interest is elevated again on C3.ai (AI) and Hertz Global (HTZ).
  • Dividend watch - Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Citizens Financial Group (CFG), Bank of Montreal (BMO), Valero (VLO), and Texas Instruments (TXN). 
  • IPO watch - Jersey Mike's (JMKE) and TicketPlus (TP)  are expected to price their IPOs and begin trading during the week. The quiet period ends on Bending Spoons (BSP), ITG (ITG), and Neutron (LIME) to free up analysts to post ratings. The IPO lockup period expires for certain blocks of shares of York Space Systems (YSS).
  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include AstraZeneca (AZN), Welltower (WELL), and Nucor (NUE).
  • All day - The three-day KBW Summer Bank Conference will begin. The conference will include presentations from many regional and community banks.
  • All day - OPEC will release its Monthly Oil Market report.
  • 8:30 a.m. The June Durable Goods Orders will be released by the U.S. Census Bureau.

 

Tuesday - July 28

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Visa (V), Coca-Cola (KO), Boeing (BA), UPS (UPS), Mondelez International (MDLZ), and Barclays (BCS). Options trading implies a double-digit move for Bloom Energy (BE) and NXP Semi (NXPI) after their reports are released.
  • All day - The two-day BTIG Annual Biotech conference will begin. Participating companies include Vaxcyte (PCVX), Compugen (CGEN), Alterity Therapeutics (ATHE), Vaxart (VXRT), Rezolute (RZLT), Avalo Therapeutics (AVTX), BullFrog AI (BFRG), and Fractyl Health (GUTS).
  • Housing reads - The S&P Case-Shiller Home Price Index and FHFA Housing Price Index will both be released to provide a clearer picture on housing price trends.
  • All day - Microsoft (MSFT) and 343 Industries' will release Halo: Campaign Evolved on Xbox Series X|S, PC, Steam, and PlayStation 5. The release is considered significant because it is the first Halo game to arrive on Sony's (SONY) PlayStation.
  • All day - The Nevada Gaming Control Board is expected to release the gaming win report for June, which will reveal if the improved trend on the Las Vegas Strip has extended for a third month,

 

Wednesday - July 29

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Microsoft (MSFT), Meta Platforms (META), Procter & Gamble (PG), Qualcomm (QCOM), Starbucks (SBUX), Fortinet (FTNT), and Arm Holdings (ARM). Options trading implies a double-digit move for Wingstop (WING), Camping World (CWH), and Lemonade (LMND) after their reports are released.
  • All day - Merck (MRK) presentations at the AIDS Conference.
  • All day - It is the closing date of the takeover bid of Allied Gold (AAUC:CA) by Zijin Gold unless it is extended.
  • 2:00 p.m. The Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision. Bank of America's base case is that the Fed will stay on hold at 3.5% to 3.75% in July, although the firm thinks the spike in oil prices has made it a close call.  
  • 2:30 p.m. Federal Reserve Chairman Kevin Warsh will hold his second press conference as the leader of the central bank.
  • 4:30 p.m. Meta Platforms (META) will hold its earnings conference call. AI spending, ad revenue growth, and capex guidance will be three major areas of investor focus. Shares of Alphabet (GOOG) and Nvidia (NVDA) have been jolted by Meta updates in the past. Options trading implies a 6% share price swing for Meta after the report drops.

 

Thursday - July 30

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Apple (AAPL), Amazon (AMZN), Mastercard (MA), Anheuser-Busch InBev (BUD), Shell (SHEL), Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), Coinbase Global (COIN), and Altria (MO). Options trading implies a double-digit move for Reddit (RDDT) and Roblox (RBLX) after their reports are released.
  • All day - Notable investor events include Ralph Lauren's (RL) annual meeting and Snap's (SNAP) annual meeting.
  • 7:00 a.m. The Bank of England will announce its interest rate decision.
  • 8:15 a.m. Etsy's (ETSY) deal to sell Depop to eBay (EBAY) for $1.2B is expected to close.
  • 8:30 a.m. The July core PCE price index report will be released. Economists forecast a 3.3% increase for the core rate in the closely watched inflation gauge.

 

Friday - July 31

  • Earnings watch - Notable companies due to report include Chevron (CVX), ExxonMobil (XOM), AbbVie (ABBV), and Moderna (MRNA).
  • All day - It is the one-year anniversary of the Figma (FIG) IPO.
  • All day - Brent crude oil futures for September expire, which could lead to extra trading volatility.
  • All day - Sony (SONY) and Marvel's Spider-Man: Brand New Day will premiere in the U.S. Current tracking points to a strong opening weekend of more than $180M at the domestic box office.
  • All day - The Black Hat USA Conference in Las Vegas begins. Notable companies participating include Nvidia (NVDA), CrowdStrike (CRWD), Microsoft (MSFT), Red Hat, SentinelOne, and TrendAI.

Seeking Alpha Editor Joshua Fineman contributed to this article.

data.title: Sensient anticipates mid-to-high teens 2026 adjusted EBITDA and EPS growth while targeting $1B natural color sales
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sensient Technologies Corporation (SXT) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “We delivered 10% local currency revenue growth, 21% local currency adjusted EBITDA growth and 26% local currency adjusted EPS growth in the second quarter,” said (Chairman, CEO, President & Member of Scientific Advisory Committee Paul Manning), adding that results were “well above our earlier projections for the year.”
  • Manning highlighted accelerating conversion demand: “The U.S. conversion to natural colors is the single largest opportunity in Sensient's history,” and said the company is “full steam ahead on this multiyear plan” to support its “$1 billion sales goal.”
  • On Color, Manning said: “During the second quarter, the Color Group invoiced approximately $25 million of natural color conversion revenue,” and added this was “in addition to the $20 million of revenue that we cumulatively invoiced through the end of Q1.”
  • (VP & CFO Tobin Tornehl) framed a key one-time item: “The company received approximately $5 million of tariff refunds in the second quarter… No additional refunds of any significance are expected in future periods,” and said the refund “resulted in approximately $0.09 benefit to EPS.”

Outlook

  • Manning updated full-year expectations: “We expect our local currency revenue to be up high single to low double digits,” and “we now expect local currency adjusted EBITDA and EPS growth in the mid-to-high teens for the year.”
  • Tornehl reiterated the shift versus prior guidance language: “We now expect local currency adjusted EBITDA and EPS to grow at a mid- to high-teen growth rates. Our previous guidance called for high single-digit to double-digit local currency adjusted growth.”
  • On investment pace and capital allocation, Manning said 2026 capex should be “between $150 million to $170 million… trending toward the top end,” while also stating, “we do not anticipate any share buybacks in the near term.”

Financial results

  • Revenue was $462.1M vs. $448.8M (analysts’ estimate).
  • Tornehl reported, “Sensient's revenue was $462.1 million in the second quarter of 2026, compared to $414.2 million in last year's second quarter,” and “Operating income was $76.7 million… compared to $57.7 million.”
  • Tornehl said cash generation improved: “Cash flow from operations was $48 million in the second quarter of 2026,” and added, “Capital expenditures were $39 million in the second quarter of 2026.”
  • On leverage and expected funding needs, Tornehl said, “Our net debt to credit adjusted EBITDA is 2.3x as of June 30, 2026,” and added, “Debt is expected to increase further with our leverage ratio entering the mid to upper 2s later in the year.”

Q&A

  • Ghansham Panjabi, Baird: asked whether customers intend to maintain color aesthetics and how technical/taste challenges are handled; (Chairman, CEO, President & Member of Scientific Advisory Committee Manning) responded that customers are “very, very keen to match the synthetic color,” and added that Sensient’s Flavors team has “taste masking platforms specific to disguising natural color off notes.”
  • Panjabi, Baird: asked about the $1B opportunity exit rate and second-half build; (Chairman, CEO, President & Member of Scientific Advisory Committee Manning) said, “We distinguish between invoice and projections,” and stated, “I would fully expect these invoiced values to rise in Q3 and to rise again in Q4.”
  • Joshua Spector, UBS: pressed on why Color’s full-year growth framing (“high teens”) appears conservative versus an accelerating conversion cadence; (Chairman, CEO, President & Member of Scientific Advisory Committee Manning) replied, “We never want to disappoint in that guidance,” while also stating, “the pace and the momentum continues to build on the natural color conversions.”
  • Spector, UBS: asked why Q2 margins were higher (excluding tariffs) while Q3 commentary implies mid-20s; (Chairman, CEO, President & Member of Scientific Advisory Committee Manning) said, “It all comes down to mix,” and added, “we feel very solidly committed to the mid-20s EBITDA margin.”
  • Lawrence Solow, CJS Securities: asked whether $25M invoiced implies ~$100M annual run-rate; (Chairman, CEO, President & Member of Scientific Advisory Committee Manning) answered, “Yes. I think your math is probably not too far off,” and added conversions could mean “the $25 million could be understating.”
  • Ming Tang, BNP Paribas: asked about upstream supply and weather risks as working capital rises; (Chairman, CEO, President & Member of Scientific Advisory Committee Manning) said, “we anticipate that there will be routinely some kind of weather event,” but added, “my goal is to never talk about ever on a call like this, a supply chain problem because we will have mitigated that.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly skeptical around Color growth cadence and margin sustainability, with UBS focusing on cadence/magnitude and incremental margins and CJS repeatedly reframing the run-rate math.
  • Management tone was positive and confident in prepared remarks and largely steady in Q&A, with Manning emphasizing “we never want to disappoint in that guidance” and repeatedly pointing to mix, invoicing visibility, and ongoing acceleration.
  • Versus Q1, management used more explicit “raise” language on full-year growth rates and spent more Q&A time on run-rate math, margin variability, and the mechanics of invoicing vs. projections.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 raised full-year profitability growth language to “mid-to-high teens” for adjusted EBITDA and EPS, compared with Q1 guidance language of “high-single to double-digit” growth.
  • The Color conversion invoicing disclosure advanced from Q1’s “about $20 million” cumulative invoiced toward the $1B goal to Q2’s additional “approximately $25 million” invoiced in the quarter.
  • Q1 commentary suggested Color profit leverage in Q2 and Q3 would be “similar” to Q1, while Q2 commentary introduced a specific Q3 margin anchor: “I also expect that the EBITDA margin for the Color Group in Q3 will be similar to prior year's Q3 EBITDA margin of 24.7%.”

Risks and concerns

  • Tornehl said, “we are constantly monitoring the situation in the Middle East,” and added the company is working “to mitigate any potential supply chain risk” tied to “fuel transportation and certain commodity prices,” including, “We have already adjusted prices where necessary.”
  • Tornehl emphasized a notable non-recurring benefit: “No additional refunds of any significance are expected in future periods,” referring to the Q2 tariff refunds.
  • Manning acknowledged raw material disruption risk while outlining mitigation: “holding working capital,” “backup alternative formulas,” and “lots of growers in both hemispheres.”

Final takeaway

Management said Q2 performance accelerated across the portfolio, led by Color and rising U.S. natural color conversion activity, while raising full-year local-currency adjusted EBITDA and EPS growth expectations to the mid-to-high teens and maintaining the $1B natural color sales target. The company emphasized that Q2 included about $5M of tariff refunds with no meaningful repeats expected, and it signaled higher capex (toward $170M) and working-capital needs to support conversion demand, with leverage expected to move into the mid-to-upper 2s later in 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
